quiz_questions.question_text: The four main topics on the AP Statistics exam are:

options.text: Exploring Data, Sampling & Experiments, Probability, Statistical Inference
Algebra, Geometry, Trigonometry, Calculus
Mean, Median, Mode, Range
Only Inference and Probability

quiz_questions.explanation: The AP Statistics framework covers these four broad content areas across the entire course.

quiz_questions.question_text: The difference between an experiment and an observational study is:

options.text: No difference
Experiments impose treatments on subjects; observational studies only observe without intervention
Observational studies are always better
Experiments are always unethical

quiz_questions.explanation: Random assignment of treatments is what distinguishes experiments from observational studies and allows causal conclusions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Randomization is important because it:

options.text: Increases bias
Reduces bias and allows causal conclusions (in experiments) or representative sampling
Makes calculations easier
Is not important

quiz_questions.explanation: Randomization is the foundation of valid inference, whether in sampling (reducing selection bias) or experiments (enabling causal claims).

quiz_questions.question_text: The normal condition for proportions requires:

options.text: np ≥ 5
np ≥ 10 AND n(1−p) ≥ 10

quiz_questions.explanation: Both np and n(1−p) must be at least 10 to ensure the normal approximation to the binomial is adequate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Type I error occurs when:

options.text: We fail to reject a false H₀
We reject H₀ when it is actually true (false positive)
The p-value is large
The CI is too narrow

quiz_questions.explanation: A Type I error is a false positive: concluding there is an effect when there truly is none.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Type II error occurs when:

options.text: We fail to reject H₀ when it is actually false (false negative)
We reject a true H₀
α is too high
The sample is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: A Type II error is a false negative: failing to detect a real effect that actually exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing sample size:

options.text: Increases Type I error rate
Decreases the margin of error and increases the power of the test
Has no effect on inference
Increases bias

quiz_questions.explanation: Larger samples give more precise estimates (smaller margin of error) and greater ability to detect real effects (higher power).

quiz_questions.question_text: To earn full credit on a free-response question, you must:

options.text: State hypotheses, check conditions, show calculations, and conclude in context
Just write the final answer
Only compute the test statistic
Only state the conclusion

quiz_questions.explanation: Each component (hypotheses, conditions, mechanics, conclusion in context) earns points on the AP rubric.

quiz_questions.question_text: Stratified sampling involves:

options.text: Sampling only the most convenient individuals
Dividing the population into strata and sampling from each to improve representation
Using only one group
Always biased sampling

quiz_questions.explanation: Stratification ensures all important subgroups are represented proportionally, often reducing variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: On the AP exam, eliminating wrong answers on multiple choice questions:

options.text: Is a waste of time
Can improve your score even when you are not sure of the correct answer
Is considered cheating
Reduces your score

quiz_questions.explanation: Process of elimination is a valuable test-taking strategy, especially since there is no penalty for guessing on the AP exam.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'interpret the confidence level' mean on the AP exam?

options.text: The parameter is definitely in the interval
If we repeated this process many times, about C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true parameter
C% of the data falls in the interval
We are C% sure the sample statistic is correct

quiz_questions.explanation: The confidence level refers to the long-run capture rate of the procedure, not the probability that any single interval contains the parameter.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the purpose of a random sample?

options.text: To ensure a large sample size
To reduce bias and allow generalization from the sample to the population
To guarantee perfect results
To avoid the need for statistics

quiz_questions.explanation: Random sampling is the basis for extending conclusions from a sample to the broader population.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'statistical power' refer to?

options.text: The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when it is false (detecting a real effect)
The probability of a Type I error
The confidence level of an interval
The p-value of the test

quiz_questions.explanation: Power = 1 − P(Type II error). Higher power means a greater chance of detecting a true effect when one exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'degrees of freedom' represent conceptually?

options.text: The sample size
The number of independent pieces of information available for estimating a parameter
The number of variables in the dataset
The significance level

quiz_questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom count the independent pieces of information remaining after estimating parameters, and they determine the shape of the t or chi-square distribution used.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between statistical significance and practical significance?

options.text: They mean the same thing
Statistical significance means the result is unlikely due to chance; practical significance means the effect is large enough to matter in the real world
Practical significance requires a larger p-value
Statistical significance is more important than practical significance

quiz_questions.explanation: A result can be statistically significant (small p-value) but practically insignificant (trivially small effect), especially with very large samples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the Welch approximation provide for a two-sample t-test?

options.text: The exact p-value
An estimated degrees of freedom when population variances are unequal
The pooled proportion
The chi-square statistic

quiz_questions.explanation: The Welch approximation calculates degrees of freedom for two-sample t-tests without assuming equal population variances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'lurking variable' mean?

options.text: A variable that is included in the model
A variable not included in the study that may affect the relationship between the variables being studied
The response variable
The explanatory variable

quiz_questions.explanation: A lurking variable is an unmeasured variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, potentially creating a misleading association.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula sheet most useful for on the AP exam?

options.text: Memorizing it entirely
Serving as a reference for specific formulas when you already know the concepts and which formula to apply
Copying it verbatim onto your answer
Relying on it solely without understanding

quiz_questions.explanation: Understanding when to use each formula is more important than memorization; the sheet helps with recall during the exam.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a complete AP Statistics preparation include?

options.text: Only practice tests
Only formula review
Conceptual understanding, formula fluency, practice problems, and time management skills
Only reading the textbook

quiz_questions.explanation: Well-rounded preparation covers conceptual understanding, procedural skill, practice under timed conditions, and strategic thinking.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the most important takeaway from AP Statistics?

options.text: Statistical reasoning and data literacy are essential life skills for making informed decisions
Only formulas matter
Statistics is only useful in math class
Always trust data without question

quiz_questions.explanation: AP Statistics teaches critical thinking about data that applies to every field and to everyday decision-making.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher wants to test whether a new fertilizer increases crop yield. She randomly assigns 60 plots to either the new fertilizer (n₁ = 30, mean = 52.3 bushels, s₁ = 6.1) or the standard fertilizer (n₂ = 30, mean = 48.7 bushels, s₂ = 5.8). She performs a two-sample t-test and gets t = 2.33 with p = 0.023. At α = 0.05, what is the correct conclusion?

options.text: Fail to reject H₀; there is no difference
Since p = 0.023 < 0.05, reject H₀. There is convincing evidence that the new fertilizer produces a higher mean crop yield than the standard fertilizer.
The test is invalid because the sample sizes are equal
Reject H₀ because t > 2

quiz_questions.explanation: p = 0.023 < α = 0.05, so we reject H₀. Because this was a randomized experiment, we can conclude the new fertilizer caused higher yields.

quiz_questions.question_text: A polling organization surveys 1,200 adults and finds that 54% approve of a new policy. They compute a 95% CI of (0.512, 0.568). A politician claims that a majority (more than 50%) supports the policy. Does the CI support this claim?

options.text: No, because the CI includes values below 54%
Yes, because the entire 95% CI (0.512 to 0.568) is above 0.50, providing convincing evidence that more than 50% of the population supports the policy
No, because we need a 99% CI to be sure
Yes, because 54% > 50%

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the entire interval (0.512 to 0.568) lies above 0.50, we have evidence at the 95% confidence level that the true population proportion exceeds 50%.

quiz_questions.question_text: A doctor conducts a study on 100 patients with two treatments. She finds p = 0.042 for the two-sample t-test. However, the mean difference is only 0.3 points on a 100-point pain scale. A colleague asks about practical significance. What is the best response?

options.text: The result is both statistically and practically significant

While the result is statistically significant (p = 0.042 < 0.05), the mean difference of 0.3 on a 100-point scale is likely too small to be clinically meaningful, illustrating the difference between statistical and practical significance
The result is not significant at all
Practical significance is irrelevant in medicine

quiz_questions.explanation: With large samples, even trivially small effects can be statistically significant. Practical significance considers whether the effect is large enough to matter in the real world.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chi-square test of independence is performed on a 3×4 contingency table with 600 observations. The test yields χ² = 15.8. What are the degrees of freedom, and is the result significant at α = 0.05 given that the critical value for df = 6 at α = 0.05 is 12.59?

options.text: df = 12; not significant
df = 6; since χ² = 15.8 > 12.59, the result is significant at α = 0.05
df = 7; significant
df = 6; not significant because 15.8 < 600

quiz_questions.explanation: df = (rows − 1)(cols − 1) = (3 − 1)(4 − 1) = 2 × 3 = 6. Since χ² = 15.8 exceeds the critical value of 12.59, we reject H₀ and conclude the variables are associated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A regression analysis of study hours (x) vs. exam score (y) yields ŷ = 45 + 4.2x, r = 0.82, r² = 0.6724. A student who studied 10 hours scored 78. What is the residual for this student?
quiz_questions.explanation: Predicted score = 45 + 4.2(10) = 45 + 42 = 87. Residual = observed − predicted = 78 − 87 = −9.0. The negative residual means the student scored 9 points below the prediction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher tests whether a coin is fair by flipping it 200 times and observing 115 heads. She uses a one-proportion z-test with H₀: p = 0.5 and Hₐ: p ≠ 0.5. She calculates z = (0.575 − 0.5)/√(0.5 × 0.5/200). What is the z-statistic?

A school administrator compares SAT math scores between students who took an AP Statistics course and those who did not. AP group (n = 85): mean = 620, s = 75. Non-AP group (n = 120): mean = 580, s = 80. She constructs a 95% CI for the difference in means (AP − Non-AP) and gets (16.2, 63.8). What does this interval tell us?

options.text: There is no difference between the groups
We are 95% confident that AP Statistics students score between 16.2 and 63.8 points higher on SAT math than non-AP students, on average
AP Statistics causes higher SAT scores
95% of AP students scored between 16.2 and 63.8 points higher

quiz_questions.explanation: The interval (16.2, 63.8) does not contain 0, suggesting a significant difference. Since this is observational, we say 'associated with' rather than 'caused.' The CI quantifies the plausible size of the difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam free-response question asks a student to design an experiment to test whether listening to music improves memory recall. Which design earns full credit?

options.text: Survey students about whether they listen to music while studying

Randomly assign 60 volunteers to two groups: one listens to music during a memorization task and the other works in silence. Both groups attempt to recall the same list. Compare mean recall scores using a two-sample t-test.
Have all students listen to music and see if they remember more than usual
Let students choose whether to listen to music or not

quiz_questions.explanation: A complete experimental design includes random assignment, a treatment (music), a control (silence), a response variable (recall score), and a statistical analysis plan.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is told that a 99% CI is (12.1, 15.9) and a 95% CI from the same data is (12.5, 15.5). She is asked to explain why the 99% interval is wider. Which explanation is correct?

options.text: The 99% interval used a larger sample
Higher confidence requires a larger critical value (z* = 2.576 vs. 1.96), which increases the margin of error and produces a wider interval
The 99% interval is wider because the standard error increased
It is wider because the sample mean changed

quiz_questions.explanation: With the same data, increasing confidence from 95% to 99% requires a larger critical value, which widens the margin of error and thus the interval. There is a trade-off between confidence and precision.

quiz_questions.question_text: A comprehensive AP Statistics review problem: A factory produces bolts with a target diameter of 10.00 mm. A sample of 40 bolts has mean 10.08 mm and s = 0.15 mm. Test at α = 0.01 whether the mean diameter differs from 10.00 mm. The t-statistic is t = (10.08 − 10.00)/(0.15/√40). What is the t-value and what should the conclusion be, given that the two-tailed critical value for df = 39 at α = 0.01 is approximately ±2.71?

options.text: t ≈ 3.37; since |3.37| > 2.71, reject H₀. There is convincing evidence at the 1% level that the mean bolt diameter differs from 10.00 mm.
t ≈ 3.37; fail to reject because 0.08 mm is a small difference
t ≈ 1.07; fail to reject H₀
t ≈ 5.33; reject H₀ at all significance levels

quiz_questions.explanation: SE = 0.15/√40 = 0.15/6.325 ≈ 0.02372. t = 0.08/0.02372 ≈ 3.37. Since |3.37| > 2.71, we reject H₀ at α = 0.01 and conclude the mean diameter significantly differs from 10.00 mm.
